name: s8_vs_u8_cast_encoding
title: (s8) sign-extends to negative addiu; (u8) zero-extends. The sb byte is the same but the addiu encoding differs.
summary:
When storing a constant byte via `sb`, the `addiu` that loads the constant has signedness baked into its encoding. `(s8)0xC3` becomes `addiu reg, $zero, -61` (encoded `0xFFC3`). `(u8)0xC3` becomes `addiu reg, $zero, 195` (encoded `0x00C3`). The `sb` produces the same byte but the addiu instruction itself does not match.

Fix: match the target's signedness when storing constants:
  - Target's addiu immediate is negative (`0xFFxx`) -> use `(s8)`.
  - Target's addiu immediate is positive (`0x00xx`) -> use `(u8)`.
proof: func_80072CD4 (commit 9abed76) had 1 byte diff for hours until the worker realized `arg2[0x4] = 0xC3` should be `arg2[0x4] = (u8)0xC3` to match target's positive addiu encoding.

structural: Function size matches and verify shows 1-2 byte diff
diff isolated to addiu instructions storing single-byte constants (anything 0x80-0xFF range)
C source uses raw hex constants without explicit (s8)/(u8) cast

rule_skeleton.instruction: Add explicit (s8) or (u8) cast to byte constants. Match target by reading the addiu immediate sign in the asm.

notes: This is most common when the value is in the 0x80-0xFF range (high bit set).
Look at the target asm `addiu` for the constant: if you see e.g. `0xFFC3`, it's signed; `0x00C3` is unsigned.
Particularly important for character codes, status flags, masks.
